title: With God on Our Side: The Struggle for Workers' Rights in a Catholic Hospital By Adam D. Reich ILR Press. 2012. 208 pages. $26 cloth
abstract: With God on our Side is a very timely and important case as there has recently been a great deal of activity around organizing health care as one of the largest and fastest growing fields in our economy. The story of how the Santa Rosa Memorial Hospital was able to organize against some fairly insurmountable obstacles is a compelling one, offering valuable experience and insight to unions wishing to organize and to scholars and students of labor struggle alike. Reich taps into a key deficiency in labor organizing and its study—the careful attention to the culture of the organization in which the attempt is being made—to illustrate just how key this element is to securing the trust of the workers, support of the community and approval—if halting—of the management. Santa Rosa Memorial Hospital (SRMH) is a Catholic hospital, founded by the Sisters of Joseph of Orange on the basis of providing quality health care to poor and indigent populations in addition to creating a measure of financial stability for the order at a time when their numbers were decreasing. As the hospital became subject to pressures that came with changes in the larger health care system, the sisters' focus increasingly moved towards aligning the hospital's mission with the ability to fight these pressures. Therefore, a new board of directors was installed and the Catholic mission was muted by a focus on customer service instead of patient care, leading to a real change in atmosphere according to the workers who hade been there for a long time. Reich does a magnificent job of illustrating these changes, beginning with the history of the order, its venture into the healthcare field, and the subtleties that led the nuns to become interested in capital much like any other organization. He shows how the very same order of nuns who were arrested with Chavez and the migrant workers came to see labor leaders in their own hospital as “greedy.”
